description: An organized idea through which a person or community understands, groups, or relates experiences and possibilities. A concept is not identical to the word that names it, the symbol that represents it, its definition, or any one example. Concepts may have disputed boundaries, change through use, and differ across traditions even when translations appear similar.
sound_symbolism: Connecting 're' leads into inward 'mo', locating the concept as a pattern of thought. 'Le' lets its relations spread to other ideas and experiences, while final 'a' opens the whole for shared examination. Four syllables suit an abstraction that can hold many instances and relations.
grammatical_notes: A countable noun: 'ha remolea ruka nai.' (This concept is complex.) It may be modified by a word, tradition, or domain in ordinary modifier-first order. Distinct from 'phelui' (word), 'relo' (symbol), 'reo' (meaning), and the definition supplied by the event noun of 'kirothe'.

pillars.secular-buddhist-philosophy: Concepts are useful patterns of thought rather than fixed essences; speakers can observe how attachment to a category shapes experience.
pillars.peace-linguistics-practices: Separating concepts from their labels helps participants discover genuine agreement beneath different words and genuine difference beneath apparent translation.
pillars.pre-industrial-wisdom: Communities may organize experience through concepts grown from local practice, landscape, craft, and memory rather than through one universal taxonomy.

tags.cognition: an organized idea used to relate or classify experience
tags.wisdom: a revisable mental pattern whose boundaries can be examined
